title: Sweat on Polyester
stainChemistry: Sweat stains are a combination of proteins (primarily albumin), lipids (sebaceous oils), salts (sodium chloride), and urea. On polyester, the protein and oil components bond to fibers over time. Yellow discoloration is caused by a reaction between aluminum in antiperspirant and proteins in sweat — this compound polymerizes with heat and becomes increasingly resistant to removal.
whyThisWorks: The treatment addresses multiple components: a bio-enzymatic (enzyme) detergent breaks down the protein (sweat is a biological/bio stain), a degreasing agent lifts the oil component, and an oxidizer addresses the yellow discoloration. For athletic polyester, a dedicated sport wash like WIN Sports Detergent or Hex Performance (technical-fabric enzyme detergent) is ideal because it combines bio-enzymatic action with odor control without coating fibers. Cold water prevents further protein polymerization in the polyester fiber.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) and tamp gently with spotting brush. Work from edge to center
2. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and work gently to remove loosened protein and digestant residue. Flush with cold water
3. If stain persists: apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) for stubborn oxidized protein — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH
5. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry — no heat
warnings: COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein

homeSolutions.agent: Polyester + sweat is the funk combo
homeSolutions.instruction: Polyester is oleophilic — it grabs and HOLDS the oils in sweat and the bacteria that feed on them. This is why workout polyester gets permanently stinky where cotton doesn't. Two battles here: the yellow pit stain (if you use antiperspirant on polyester) and the lingering odor (from oil-trapped bacteria). You'll treat them separately.

homeSolutions.agent: Skip fabric softener from now on
homeSolutions.instruction: Fabric softener coats polyester fiber with a waxy film that traps sweat oils and bacteria. If you've been using softener on workout gear, that's most of your odor problem — stop using it going forward. Do NOT use softener in this treatment load.

Fill a sink or bucket with 2 gallons of cold water and add 1 cup of white vinegar. Soak the garment for 30 minutes — longer if it's been smelly for a while. Vinegar's mild acid kills the odor-causing bacteria and breaks the aluminum-protein bond behind yellow pit stains. It also neutralizes residual alkaline detergent left in the fiber from previous washes.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + cold water on visible pit stains
homeSolutions.instruction: Lift the garment out of the vinegar soak. On any visible yellow or gray pit stains, apply a small drop of Dawn directly, work it in with a fingertip for 30 seconds, let sit 5 minutes. Dawn cuts the oil component that's trapping the protein. Blot with a clean damp cloth; don't rinse yet.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme pretreat for the protein (20–30 min)
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply an enzyme stain remover — OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout — per the label. Let it dwell 20–30 minutes. Enzymes digest the sweat protein (albumin) that's bonded to the polyester. This step does most of the odor work for set-in funk.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Don't mix enzyme products with chlorine bleach — it deactivates the enzymes. Skip OxiClean if the polyester has any acetate blend (check care tag); safe on pure polyester.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold machine wash — sport detergent if you have it
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on cold with a technical-fabric detergent if you have one (WIN Sports Detergent, Hex Performance, Nathan Sport Wash, or any "stink-busting" detergent). Regular detergent works too but leaves more residue on polyester. Add a half-cup of white vinegar to the rinse cycle. Absolutely no fabric softener in this wash.

homeSolutions.agent: Air dry — never tumble-dry athletic polyester
homeSolutions.instruction: Hang or lay flat to air-dry. Tumble-drying polyester at any temperature sets residual stink and can shrink technical fabric. Outdoor drying in direct sun is even better — UV neutralizes some of the residual odor compounds. On activewear, this also extends the life of the fabric's performance features.

If the garment still smells after air-drying, or the pit stain is heavy and heat-set, try one more round (steps 3–6). If it still doesn't clear, be honest with yourself: set-in odor and yellow stains on polyester sometimes can't be recovered — the oils have permanently bonded. Tell a pro cleaner: "Sweat + deodorant on polyester athletic wear, treated with vinegar soak + Dawn + enzyme, cold wash, no softener, no heat." Expect partial, not full, removal on heavily set items.

steps: 1. Rinse immediately with COLD water. Blot with a clean white cloth — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup cold water. Apply to stain and blot
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat soap application until stain fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all soap residue
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer or any heat until stain is completely gone
products: sport wash (WIN or Hex Performance) for athletic polyester
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional if the stain has been heat-set, if multiple home treatments haven't improved it, or if the fabric is delicate/expensive
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Do NOT use OxiClean or oxidizing agents on protein stains — they can set the stain permanently

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is sweat on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
